q: A ball is thrown up with a certain velocity so that it reaches a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Find the ratio of the two different times of the ball reaching <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> in both the directions.
question: A ball is thrown up with a certain velocity so that it reaches a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Find the ratio of the two different times of the ball reaching <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>h</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> in both the directions.
solution:
u=2gh
Now,

S=h3, a=-g

S=ut+12at2
h3=2ght+12-gt2
t2g2-2ght+h3=0

From quadratic equation

t1, t2=2gh±2gh-4 g2h3 g

t1t2=2gh-4gh32gh+4gh3

=3-23+2

subject: Physics
q: A passenger sitting in a train A moving at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> observes another train B moving in the opposite direction for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the velocity of the train B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then length of train B is:
question: A passenger sitting in a train A moving at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> observes another train B moving in the opposite direction for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If the velocity of the train B is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then length of train B is:
solution:
Let the velocity of A be vA and the velocity of B be vB.

The relative velocity is

v→BA =v→B-v→A⇒vBA= 54-(-90)=144 km h-1

⇒vBA= 40 m s-1

Time=lengthvBA 

⇒length= 40 m s-1 × 8 s=320 m

Ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is released from the top at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. Ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is thrown vertically down with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. After a certain time, both balls meet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the ground. Find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: Two balls <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are placed at the top of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> tall tower. Ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is released from the top at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. Ball <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is thrown vertically down with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. After a certain time, both balls meet <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above the ground. Find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. [use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Displacement covered by first ball,180-100=0+12×10×t2   ⇒t=4 s

Now, the second body gets only,  t2=4-2=2 s.

Displacement covered by second will be same,

80=u×2+12×10×22

⇒u=80-202=30 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A particle moves in a straight line so that its displacement $x$ at any time $t$ is given by $x^2=1+t^2$. Its acceleration at any time $\mathrm{t}$ is $x^{-\mathrm{n}}$ where $\mathrm{n}=$ ___________
question: A particle moves in a straight line so that its displacement $x$ at any time $t$ is given by $x^2=1+t^2$. Its acceleration at any time $\mathrm{t}$ is $x^{-\mathrm{n}}$ where $\mathrm{n}=$ ___________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2=1+\mathrm{t}^2 \\ & 2 \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{xv}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{v} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=1 \\ & \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{v}^2=1 \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{1-\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{1-\mathrm{t}^2 / \mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^3}=\mathrm{x}^{-3}\end{aligned}$

q: A ball is thrown vertically up (taken as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> ) from the ground. The correct momentum-height <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mo>)</mo></math> diagram is:
question: A ball is thrown vertically up (taken as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> ) from the ground. The correct momentum-height <math><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mo>)</mo></math> diagram is:
solution:
If the ball was thrown with initial velocity u , then its velocity at height 'h' can be written as.
v=u2-2gh
Its linear momentum, p=mv=mu2-2gh
Or  p2=m2u2-2m2gh
The graph between p & h will be a parabola.
In motion when particle was going up, its velocity is positive and decreasing with height and so is the linear momentum. When it reaches the maximum height, the velocity and linear momentum will be zero. Again, it will start to fall back, the velocity and linear momentum will increase but will have negative value.

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The ratio of velocity after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____. {take, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>}
question: A ball is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The ratio of velocity after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____. {take, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>}
solution:
The formula to calculate the velocity of the ball after 3 s is given by

v3=u-3g   ...1

The formula to calculate the velocity of the ball after 5 s is given by

v5=u-5g   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) to obtain the required ratio.

v3v5=u-3gu-5g   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required ratio.

v3v5=150-30150-50=120100=65= 5+15

Comparing the above equation with the given expression, it can be written that x=5.

q:
Two spherical balls having equal masses with radius of 5 cm each are thrown upwards along the same vertical direction at an interval of 3 s with the same initial velocity of 35 m s-1, then these balls collide at a height of _____m,

(take g=10 m s-2)
question:
Two spherical balls having equal masses with radius of 5 cm each are thrown upwards along the same vertical direction at an interval of 3 s with the same initial velocity of 35 m s-1, then these balls collide at a height of _____m,

(take g=10 m s-2)
solution:
S1=S2

35t+12(−g)t2=35(t−3)+12(−g)(t−3)2

35t−12gt2=35t−35×3−12gt2−6t+9

35×3+45=30t

t=15030=5

height h=35×5−12×10×52

h=175−125=50 m

q: An object is allowed to fall from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> above the earth, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of earth. Its velocity when it strikes the earth’s surface, ignoring air resistance, will be :
question: An object is allowed to fall from a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> above the earth, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of earth. Its velocity when it strikes the earth’s surface, ignoring air resistance, will be :
solution:
As gravitational field is a conservative field and therefore mechanical energy will be conserved. Therefore,
Loss in PE = Gain in KE

-GMm2R--GMmR=12mv2

⇒v2=GMR=gRas g=GMR2

⇒v=gR

q: The displacement and the increase in the velocity of a moving particle in the time interval of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. The distance travelled by the particle in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mtext>th </mtext></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: The displacement and the increase in the velocity of a moving particle in the time interval of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, respectively. The distance travelled by the particle in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mtext>th </mtext></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is ___________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Considering acceleration is constant, we can write

v=u+at

As increase in velocity in 1 s is given as 50 m s-1, therefore

⇒a=50 m s-2

Now, displacement covered between given time interval is, 

125=ut+12at2

⇒125=u+a2

⇒u=100 m s-1

Velocity after 1 s will be, v=100+50=150 m s-1

Therefore, displacement covered in t+1 s to t+2 s will 

∴Snth=vt'+12at'2, where t'=1 s

=175 m

subject: Physics
q: The velocity $(v)$ - Distance $(x)$ graph is shown in figure. Which graph represents acceleration $(a)$ versus distance $(x)$ variation of this system?
question: The velocity $(v)$ - Distance $(x)$ graph is shown in figure. Which graph represents acceleration $(a)$ versus distance $(x)$ variation of this system?
solution:
The v-x graph shows velocity decreasing linearly with distance. Using kinematic equation $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2ax$, we can establish the relationship between acceleration and position.
From the linear v-x relationship, differentiating using $a = v\frac{dv}{dx}$ yields acceleration that is linearly proportional to distance from the origin.
The a-x graph shows a straight line passing through the origin with positive slope, indicating that acceleration increases linearly with distance while remaining positive throughout the motion.

q: An automobile, travelling at $40 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, can be stopped at a distance of $40 \mathrm{~m}$ by applying brakes. If the same automobile is travelling at $80 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, the minimum stopping distance, in metres, is (assume no skidding)
question: An automobile, travelling at $40 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, can be stopped at a distance of $40 \mathrm{~m}$ by applying brakes. If the same automobile is travelling at $80 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, the minimum stopping distance, in metres, is (assume no skidding)
solution:
According to question, $u_1=40 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}, v_1=0$ and $s_1=40 \mathrm{~m}$ using $v^2-u^2=2 a s ; 0^2-40^2=2 a \times 40$..(i) Again, $0^2-80^2=2$ as From eqn. (i) and (ii)
Stopping distance, $s=160 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q: A boy pushes a box of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> with a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>20</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> on a frictionless surface. If the box was initially at rest, then _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is displacement along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>
question: A boy pushes a box of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> with a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>20</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> on a frictionless surface. If the box was initially at rest, then _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is displacement along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>
solution:
F→=20i^+10j^

a→=F→m=20i^+10j^2⇒10i^+5j^

∴ s→=12a→t2=1210i^+5j^×102

⇒5010i^+5j^m

∴ Displacement along x-axis

⇒50×10⇒500 m

subject: Physics
q: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from the top of a tower. The ball strikes the ground after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The height from the ground up to which the ball can rise will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is _____ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> from the top of a tower. The ball strikes the ground after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The height from the ground up to which the ball can rise will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is _____ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Initial velocity of ball is u=19.6 m s-1, final velocity v=0.

Using v=u+at, here, acceleration a=-g.

Time taken in upward motion above tower is ta=ug=19.69.8=2 s

Now, time taken from top most point to ground is td=6-2=4 s.

Or, td=2hmaxg    (Using s=ut+12gt2)

⇒hmax=16×9.82=3925 m.

Hence, the value of k=392.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Area under velocity-time graph gives the distance travelled by the body in a given time.
Statement II: Area under acceleration-time graph is equal to the change in velocity in the given time.
In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Area under velocity-time graph gives the distance travelled by the body in a given time.
Statement II: Area under acceleration-time graph is equal to the change in velocity in the given time.
In the light of given statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is correct but Statement II is false
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is true
solution:
The definition of displacement is given by the product of velocity and time.

∫vdt=∆x=displacement of body in given time.

Distance is a scalar quantity and displacement is a vector quantity defined by the shortest distance between the initial and the final position.

Acceleration is defined by the rate of change of velocity.
∫adt=∆v=change in velocity in given time.

Thus, the area under the velocity time graph gives the displacement of the body and the area under the acceleration time graph gives the change in velocity of the body.

subject: Physics
q: From the top of a tower, a ball is thrown vertically upward which reaches the ground in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. A second ball thrown vertically downward from the same position with the same speed reaches the ground in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. A third ball released, from the rest from the same location, will reach the ground in _____ s.
question: From the top of a tower, a ball is thrown vertically upward which reaches the ground in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. A second ball thrown vertically downward from the same position with the same speed reaches the ground in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. A third ball released, from the rest from the same location, will reach the ground in _____ s.
solution:
When it is thrown upward, -h=v×6-12g62

When it is thrown downward, -h=-v×1.5-12g1.52

Therefore, 5h=536+9 h=45 m

When the ball is dropped, h=12gt2⇒45=12×10×t2⇒t=3 s

Alternate method, t=t1t2=6×1.5=3 s

subject: Physics
q: Water droplets are coming from an open tap at a particular rate. The spacing between a droplet observed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> second after its fall to the next droplet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>34</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. At what rate the droplets are coming from the tap ? (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Water droplets are coming from an open tap at a particular rate. The spacing between a droplet observed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> second after its fall to the next droplet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>34</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. At what rate the droplets are coming from the tap ? (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
In 4th sec, 1st drop will travel 

⇒12×9.8×42=78.4 m

∴ 2nd drop would have travelled

⇒78.4-34.3=44.1 m

Time for 2nd drop

129.8t2=44.1

t=3 sec

∴ Each drop have time gap of 1 sec.

∴ 1 drop per second.

subject: Physics
q: The graph of an object's motion (along the $x-$ axis) is shown in the figure. The instantaneous velocity of the object at points $A$ and $B$ are $v_A$ and $v_B$ respectively. Then
question: The graph of an object's motion (along the $x-$ axis) is shown in the figure. The instantaneous velocity of the object at points $A$ and $B$ are $v_A$ and $v_B$ respectively. Then
solution: Instantaneous velocity $v=\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ From graph, $v_A=\frac{\Delta x_A}{\Delta t_A}=\frac{4 m}{8 s}=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $v_B=\frac{\Delta x_B}{\Delta t_B}=\frac{8 m}{16 s}=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ i.e., $v_A=v_B=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: A train starting from rest first accelerates uniformly up to a speed of $80 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ for time $t$, then it moves with a constant speed for time $3 t$. The average speed of the train for this duration of journey will be (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}$ ) :
question: A train starting from rest first accelerates uniformly up to a speed of $80 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ for time $t$, then it moves with a constant speed for time $3 t$. The average speed of the train for this duration of journey will be (in $\mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}$ ) :
solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Average speed } & =\frac{\text { total distance }}{\text { time taken }} \\ & =\frac{\frac{80 \times \mathrm{t}}{2}+80 \times 3 \mathrm{t}}{4 \mathrm{t}}=70 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr} .\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A vehicle travels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and another <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then its average speed is :
question: A vehicle travels <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and another <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> with speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then its average speed is :
solution:
Average speed in this case is given by, 2vav=1v1+1v2

⇒2vav=13+15

⇒2vav=815

⇒vav=154=3.75 km h-1

subject: Physics
q: A body falling from rest under gravity passes a certain point $P$. It was at a distance of $400 \mathrm{~m}$ from $P, 4 \mathrm{~s}$ prior to passing through $P$. If $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, then the height above the point $\mathrm{P}$ from where the body began to fall is
question: A body falling from rest under gravity passes a certain point $P$. It was at a distance of $400 \mathrm{~m}$ from $P, 4 \mathrm{~s}$ prior to passing through $P$. If $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, then the height above the point $\mathrm{P}$ from where the body began to fall is
solution: We have $h=\frac{1}{2}{g t^2}^2$ and $h+400=\frac{1}{2} g(t+4)^2$. Subtracting we get $400=8 \mathrm{~g}+4 \mathrm{gt}$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=8 \mathrm{sec} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{h}=\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 64=320 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \therefore \text { Desired height }=320+400=720 \mathrm{~m} . \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its velocity is increasing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> per meter. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a point where its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A particle is moving in a straight line such that its velocity is increasing at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> per meter. The acceleration of the particle is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a point where its velocity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
According to the question, velocity is increasing at 5 m s-1. Therefore, dvds=5 s-1. 

Now acceleration a=dvdt=vdvds

⇒a=vdvds=20×5=100 m s-2

subject: Physics
q: The position, velocity and acceleration of a particle moving with a constant acceleration can be represented by :
question: The position, velocity and acceleration of a particle moving with a constant acceleration can be represented by :
solution: Represent correct graph for a particle moving with constant acceleration, as for constant acceleration velocity-time graph is a straight line with positive slope, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>t</mi></math> graph should be an opening upward parabola.

subject: Physics
q: A balloon was moving upwards with a uniform velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. An object of finite mass is dropped from the balloon when it was at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the ground level. The height of the balloon from the ground when object strikes the ground was around: (takes the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A balloon was moving upwards with a uniform velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. An object of finite mass is dropped from the balloon when it was at a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the ground level. The height of the balloon from the ground when object strikes the ground was around: (takes the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Object is projected as shown so as per motion under gravity, 

S=ut+12at2

-75=+10t+12(-10)t2⇒t=5 s

Object takes t=5 s to fall on ground height of balloon from ground, 

H=75+ut

=75+10×5=125 m
